speaker: Ram Dass
text: During the mid part of the 20th century, the steady stream of visitors traveled to the ancient land of Tamil Nadu in the southern region of India

speaker: Ram Dass
text: to experience the remarkable presence and clear teachings of Sri Ramana Maharshi.

speaker: Ram Dass
text: The Maharshi's name needs little introduction.

speaker: Ram Dass
text: For then, as now, seekers who wish to awaken from the powerful illusion of the ego and abide in the perfect peace of their true nature have turned to his teachings.

speaker: Ram Dass
text: There was a spontaneous simplicity and humility about him, a sense of universally quality and a heart-melting love.

speaker: Ram Dass
text: His actions were natural and spontaneous and reflected a life that was fully identified with the one consciousness, the self in all beings.

speaker: Ram Dass
text: Card Jung had planned to visit the Maharshi but for various reasons never made it.

speaker: Ram Dass
text: Jung writes about Ramana.

speaker: Ram Dass
text: What we find in the life and teachings of Sri Ramana is the purest of India with its breath of world liberated and liberating humanity.

speaker: Ram Dass
text: It is a chant of millenniums.

speaker: Ram Dass
text: In India, he is the white of spot in a white space.

speaker: Ram Dass
text: Born in 1879 to Pious parents, the child was named Venkataraman.

speaker: Ram Dass
text: Aside from several auspicious indications surrounding his birth, there were few specific signs that the young boys destiny would take a spiritual turn.

speaker: Ram Dass
text: When he was only 16 years old, attending high school in the city of Madurai, in the south Indian state of Tamil Nadu,

speaker: Ram Dass
text: he had a remarkable experience of death which led to a permanent awakening to the deathless spirit.

speaker: Ram Dass
text: Arriving at the Sacred Hill, Arunachala, he spent the next 54 years either on its slopes or foothills.

speaker: Ram Dass
text: People from all over the world came to sit in his presence, which had the profound effect of quieting the mind and providing glimpses of the perfect peace which is our natural state.

speaker: Ram Dass
text: The Maharshi's teachings are uniquely suited to modern life and provide, reclear and balanced synthesis of head.

speaker: Ram Dass
text: He was not concerned with theory or philosophical discussion.

speaker: Ram Dass
text: Rather, he consistently guided the seeker back to the source of real being with its theoretical basis in Advaita or Nanduality.

speaker: Ram Dass
text: Ramana taught that we exist as the supreme self at all times.

speaker: Ram Dass
text: We need only awake into this reality by seeking the source of the ego or I thought and abide in the self that we always are.

speaker: Ram Dass
text: He referred to this method as self-inquiry.

speaker: Ram Dass
text: The path of self-inquiry frees one from the unceasing fear and turmoil, resulting from taking the ego to be real.

speaker: Ram Dass
text: By becoming free of the ego illusion, one experiences true freedom and supreme peace.

speaker: Ram Dass
text: It is this path that takes one from the apparent duality of the individual and the world to the bliss of one's real nature.

speaker: Ram Dass
text: Through this awakening to self-awareness, even by imperfect glimpses, one begins to sense a reality not confined to the ego's world.

speaker: Ram Dass
text: And this current of awareness is ultimately revealed as the self pure consciousness.

speaker: Ram Dass
text: The Maharishi generally taught in silence and the sincere seeker would often experience a silent influence in the heart.

speaker: Ram Dass
text: Verbal expression of this silence was always given freely to all and frequently combined with humor and laughter.

speaker: Ram Dass
text: While his physical presence was overwhelming and his majesty and beauty indescribable, he remained completely natural, simple.

speaker: Ram Dass
text: Unassuming and wholly unaffected, join me as we explore the life and teachings of Ramana Maharishi and turn back to the source of abiding happiness, the source in which all questions and doubts resolve themselves in the awareness of our true.

speaker: Ram Dass
text: And this is where the story begins. For a few people began to gather around the young sage seeking his guidance.

speaker: Ram Dass
text: Perhaps it was simply the fulfillment of bodily karma, or the motiveless compassion that reacted to the desire for guidance on the part of those sincere secrets.

speaker: Ram Dass
text: But something seemed to draw him back to a fuller bodily life.

speaker: Ram Dass
text: Slowly, seekers would approach him to have a doubt cleared, or to simply sit in his peaceful presence.

speaker: Ram Dass
text: Shiva Purkasham, Pile was one of the early visitors who became captivated by the young sage.

speaker: Ram Dass
text: The teachings on self-increase as given to Shiva Purkasham Pile became the key message of the Maharishi.

speaker: Ram Dass
text: The great Sanskrit scholar and ascetic, Vashisstha Ganapati Muni, had mastered a broad range of scriptures, disciplines and meditation practices.

speaker: Ram Dass
text: Feeling frustrated that he had not progressed further in experience. Ganapati remembered the young sage living on the hill and approached him with great emotion,

speaker: Ram Dass
text: desiring to know the true meaning of tapas or spiritual practice. The young sage gazed at him silently for a while, and then replied,

speaker: Ram Dass
text: If one watches whenst the notion I arises, the mind is absorbed into that, that is tapas.

speaker: Ram Dass
text: When a mantra is repeated, if one watches the source from which the mantra sound is produced, the mind is absorbed in that, that is tapas.

speaker: Ram Dass
text: This response on the part of the young ascetic filled Ganapati Muni with joy, and he declared that then Kattaraman should now be known as Bhadvan Sri Ramana Mahash.

speaker: Ram Dass
text: Those who surrounded Ramana during his lifetime came from very diverse cultural and social backgrounds.

speaker: Ram Dass
text: What they had in common was a sincere aspiration to experience true inner peace and freedom.

speaker: Ram Dass
text: Ramana never saw anyone as separate from himself and had no disciples in the conventional sense.

speaker: Ram Dass
text: He told a visitor in 1936, the person may call himself my disciple or devotee.

speaker: Ram Dass
text: I do not consider anyone to be my disciple. If people call themselves my disciples, I do not approve or disapprove. In my view, all are alike.

speaker: Ram Dass
text: What can I say to them? He regularly said that the guru was not the physical form and guidance continues after the demise of the body.

speaker: Ram Dass
text: Therefore, there was no need to create a lineage or provide for transmission in order to carry on successorship.

speaker: Ram Dass
text: Several well-known devotees expressed the different ways in which they experienced Mahashis, Presence and Teachings.

speaker: Ram Dass
text: The inquiry is not a mental investigation, such as psychologist might indulge in. It is not approving into the faculties, urges, memories or tendencies of one's conscious or subconscious mind.

speaker: Ram Dass
text: But a quest of the pure I amness that lies behind all these. To meet the needs of various seekers, Ramana did expound various doctrines.

speaker: Ram Dass
text: But we have heard him say that his true teaching firmly based on his own experience is Ajata, that which was never born.

speaker: Ram Dass
text: The most touching sight is the number of tiny children up to about seven years of age, who climb the mountain all on their own to come and sit near Mahashis,

speaker: Ram Dass
text: even though he may not speak a word or hardly look at them for days together. They do not play, but just sit quietly there in perfect contentment.

speaker: Ram Dass
text: You are the self, Ramana tells us, nothing but the self. Anything else is just imagination. So be the self here and now.

speaker: Ram Dass
text: There is no need to run off to a forest or shut oneself in a room, carry on with your essential activities, but free yourself from association with the doer of them.

speaker: Ram Dass
text: Self is the witness. You are that.

speaker: Ram Dass
text: Bhagavan has written a song whose import is that the path is very easy. If the mind is not allowed to stray after the senses and directed to inquire into its true nature, certainly the heart will be reached and the self perceived.

speaker: Ram Dass
text: The teaching of Bhagavan has acquired worldwide recognition and attracted earnest seekers from all five continents as much for its simplicity as for its sturdy rationality, which appeal both to the head and the heart.

speaker: Ram Dass
text: It can, however, be summed up in the ancient dictum, no thyself or seek the seeker. When Ramana spoke, the word seemed to come out of an abyss. One could see immaculate purity and non-attachment in him and his movements.

speaker: Ram Dass
text: In his vicinity the minds distractions were overpowered by an austere and potent calmness and the unique bliss of peace was directly experienced.

speaker: Ram Dass
text: Again and again he gave us the teaching that the real Maharshi was not the body which people saw. It was the inner being. Those who never made the journey to India during his lifetime may take comfort in this thought.

speaker: Ram Dass
text: That it is possible to invoke his presence wherever they are and to feel its reality in the heart.

speaker: Ram Dass
text: Ramana wrote very little and taught mainly through the tremendous power of silence. The writings of Ramana were created mostly to meet the specific needs of others and fall into two general categories.

speaker: Ram Dass
text: The first is where perfect knowledge is combined with the ecstasy of devotion.

speaker: Ram Dass
text: Tears of joy streamed down his face as he wrote them. These devotional poems mirror the aspect of longing and the bliss of fulfillment.

speaker: Ram Dass
text: The other writings enunciate his teachings in both poetry and prose. These include clear expositions of the nature of God, the mind, the world, and the individual.

speaker: Ram Dass
text: Just as Ramana realized the self without previous spiritual of philosophical instruction, he attached little importance to theoretical study.

speaker: Ram Dass
text: Therefore his writings focus on the practical approach of turning toward the source of self knowledge rather than getting lost in the never-ending cycle of intellectual.

speaker: Ram Dass
text: Ramana indicated that the heart was another name for God or the self. It is called heart since it is the source from which the universe rises.

speaker: Ram Dass
text: He stated the heart is not physical. In Sanskrit, heart is Fridaya, which means that which is the center.

speaker: Ram Dass
text: Is that from which thoughts arise on which they subsist and where they are resolved? The heart is the center of it all. It is said to be the infant.

speaker: Ram Dass
text: In the book, Ramana Arunachala, Arthur Osborne writes, it was the most majestic film I've ever seen, the most awe inspiring.

speaker: Ram Dass
text: And yet without incident, a view of the Arunachala hill from the Ashram Drive and then a tall, frail, light-complexion man with short white hair, descending the slope of the hill with the aid of a staff.

speaker: Ram Dass
text: Then he was seen coming out of the Ashram Hall, stopping to smile to a baby, walking across the Ashram ground, just simple, everyday actions, the simplicity was so natural, so spontaneous.

speaker: Ram Dass
text: After years of living in caves upon the Arunachala hill, the Maharshi moved down to its base, near the resting place of his mother. After a short time, a small Ashramah began to take shape around him.

speaker: Ram Dass
text: And what is now the current Sri Ramana Arunachala, had its modest beginning. Seekers from all backgrounds and religions came to be in his presence.

speaker: Ram Dass
text: Ramana sat in a modest hall of available day and night to answer questions from sincere seekers. His only possessions were a loincloth and a towel. He never asked anything from anyone.

speaker: Ram Dass
text: Until the frailty of age set in, there were no set hours for approaching him. During the final year of his life, devotees were requested to let him rest at midday. In response to this request by Ashram authorities, Ramana himself sat outside the hall until the rule was retracted. His humanity and sense of equality was due universal.

speaker: Ram Dass
text: In 1949, it was detected that the Maharshi had malignant sarcoma in his left arm. In spite of intense medical care, on April 14, 1950, his physical end was apparent. The last photo of Ramana was by the French photographer, Henri Cacier Brisson, taken just ten days before the Maharshi left the body.

speaker: Ram Dass
text: In the evening, as devotees sat on the terrace outside the room built specially for his convenience during this last illness.

speaker: Ram Dass
text: They spontaneously began to sing the refrain to one of his stirring hymns to Arunachala. Arthur Osborn writes about that evening, on hearing it, Ramana's eyes open and shown. He gave a brief smile of indescribable tenderness.

speaker: Ram Dass
text: From the outer corner of his eyes tears of bliss rolled down, one more deep breath. At that very moment, 847 pm, an enormous star trailed slowly across the sky passing to the northeast of the peak of Arunachala.

speaker: Ram Dass
text: The meteor was noted as far away as Bombay.
